content:
# Leviticus Chapter 20 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70r–70v
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 106–107
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_020.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 20 is the penal companion to the prohibition catalogue of chapter 18, completing the legal architecture of the Holiness Code's central diptych. Where chapter 18 enumerated the forbidden acts — child sacrifice to Molech, illicit sexual unions, recourse to mediums and necromancers — chapter 20 assigns the penalties. The chapter is structured in a ring: it opens and closes with the topic of necromancy/mediumship (vv. 6, 27), frames the central penal code within holiness exhortations (vv. 7–8; 22–26), and advances from the most public crime (Molech-worship, requiring communal stoning) through private sexual violations, before closing with the theological vision of a distinguished people in a gift-land.

The dominant legal formula is the doubled **mot yumat** — the Niphal of *muth* intensified by its own Qal infinitive absolute: "dying he shall die," rendered throughout as "shall surely be put to death." This formula appears nine times in the chapter (vv. 2, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 15, 16, 27), marking it as the most death-formula-dense chapter in all of Leviticus. The formula **damav bo / demeihem bam** — "his/their blood is upon him/them" — serves as the rhetorical and forensic seal on each clause: the condemned person's death is their own doing; those who execute bear no guilt.

The chapter's graduated penalty structure is theologically precise. Capital offenses receiving **mot yumat** include: Molech-worship (v. 2), cursing parents (v. 9), adultery (v. 10), incest with father's wife (v. 11), incest with daughter-in-law (v. 12), male-male intercourse (v. 13), bestiality (vv. 15–16), and mediumship/necromancy (v. 27). A secondary tier receives **karet** — divine cutting-off from the people: consulting mediums (v. 6), incest with a sister (v. 17), intercourse during menstrual impurity (v. 18). A third tier prescribes **childlessness** (aririn / arurim): intercourse with an uncle's wife (v. 20) or a brother's wife (v. 21). Incest with aunts by blood (v. 19) receives the vague "shall bear their iniquity" — a divine consequence without specified human execution.

The chapter's closing theology (vv. 22–26) is its most concentrated: the triple use of the root *badal* ("to separate/distinguish") in vv. 24–26 forms an explicit theological argument — God's act of election-separation grounds Israel's obligation to practice dietary distinction, which in turn maintains the people's status as the "distinguished/holy" people in covenant with a holy God. The land-promise formula ("a land flowing with milk and honey," v. 24) stands at the exact hinge between expelling the nations and the call to separation: the gift and the demand are one and the same covenantal reality.

**Key term rulings:** YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" | ger = "sojourner" | mot yumat = "shall surely be put to death" | ervah = "nakedness" (preserve "uncover nakedness") | to'evah = "abomination" | zimmah = "depravity" | tevel = "perversion" | chesed (v. 17) = "disgrace" | niddah = "impurity" (v. 21) | aririn/arurim = "childless" | hivdil/havdalah = "separated/distinguish" | karet = "cut off" | am ha-aretz = "people of the land"

---

## Consensus Translation

**20:1** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**20:2** "Say to the sons of Israel: Any man of the sons of Israel or of the sojourners who sojourn in Israel who gives any of his children to Molech shall surely be put to death; the people of the land shall stone him with stones.

**20:3** I myself will set my face against that man and will cut him off from among his people, because he has given one of his children to Molech, defiling my sanctuary and profaning my holy name.

**20:4** And if the people of the land close their eyes to that man when he gives one of his children to Molech, and do not put him to death,

**20:5** then I will set my face against that man and against his family, and I will cut him off from among his people — him and all who go whoring after him, whoring after Molech.

**20:6** And should a person turn to the mediums and to the spirits of the dead, to go whoring after them, I will set my face against that person and will cut him off from among his people.

**20:7** And you shall sanctify yourselves and be holy, for I am the LORD your God.

**20:8** And you shall keep my statutes and do them. I am the LORD who sanctifies you.

**20:9** For anyone who curses his father or his mother shall surely be put to death; he has cursed his father or his mother — his blood is upon him.

**20:10** If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor, both the adulterer and the adulteress shall surely be put to death.

**20:11** If a man lies with his father's wife, he has uncovered his father's nakedness; both of them shall surely be put to death — their blood is upon them.

**20:12** If a man lies with his daughter-in-law, both of them shall surely be put to death; they have committed a perversion — their blood is upon them.

**20:13** If a man lies with a male as with a woman, both of them have committed an abomination; they shall surely be put to death — their blood is upon them.

**20:14** If a man takes a woman and her mother also, it is depravity; he and they shall be burned with fire, that there may be no depravity in your midst.

**20:15** If a man lies with an animal, he shall surely be put to death, and you shall kill the animal.

**20:16** If a woman approaches any animal so that it lies with her, you shall kill the woman and the animal; they shall surely be put to death — their blood is upon them.

**20:17** If a man takes his sister — a daughter of his father or a daughter of his mother — and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness, it is a disgrace; and they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people. He has uncovered the nakedness of his sister — he shall bear his iniquity.

**20:18** If a man lies with a woman during her menstrual impurity and uncovers her nakedness, he has laid bare her flow, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood — both of them shall be cut off from among their people.

**20:19** And the nakedness of your mother's sister or of your father's sister you shall not uncover, for that is to lay bare one's own flesh; they shall bear their iniquity.

**20:20** If a man lies with his uncle's wife, he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness; they shall bear their sin and shall die childless.

**20:21** If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity; he has uncovered his brother's nakedness — they shall be childless.

**20:22** And you shall keep all my statutes and all my rules and do them, that the land where I am bringing you to live may not vomit you out.

**20:23** And you shall not walk in the statutes of the nation that I am driving out before you, for they did all these things, and I detested them.

**20:24** But I have said to you: You shall inherit their land, and I will give it to you to possess, a land flowing with milk and honey. I am the LORD your God, who has separated you from the peoples.

**20:25** You shall therefore distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean, and between the unclean bird and the clean. You shall not make yourselves detestable by animal or by bird or by anything that creeps on the ground, which I have set apart for you as unclean.

**20:26** And you shall be holy to me, for I the LORD am holy and have separated you from the peoples, that you should be mine.

**20:27** A man or a woman in whom there is a medium or a spirit of the dead shall surely be put to death; they shall be stoned with stones — their blood is upon them."

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Address formula.** EMET: "spoke to Moshe." LOGOS: "spoke to Moses." PNEUMA: "spoke to Moses." Consensus: "Moses" — English name form per project convention, consistent throughout Genesis, Exodus, and Leviticus series. EMET's "Moshe" is appropriate for a hyper-literal register but is not the project standard for the consensus text.

**V. 2 — "Any man of the sons of Israel or of the sojourners."** EMET: "Any man from the sons of Yisra'el and from the sojourner [ha-ger] who sojourns in Yisra'el." LOGOS: "Any man of the sons of Israel or of the sojourners who sojourn in Israel." PNEUMA: "Any person — whether Israelite or foreigner living in Israel." Consensus: LOGOS's rendering — "Any man of the sons of Israel or of the sojourners who sojourn in Israel." The MT text reads *ish ish mi-bney yisrael u-min ha-ger ha-gar be-yisrael* — formally "man man from the sons of Israel and from the sojourner who sojourns in Israel." LOGOS renders this most naturally while preserving the legal scope. PNEUMA's "any person" loses the *ish* (man) and softens the text's framing; EMET's transliterations ("Yisra'el") are not the consensus convention.

**V. 2 — "Children" vs. "seed."** EMET: "gives from his seed [zar'o]." LOGOS: "gives any of his children." PNEUMA: "gives his children." Consensus: "gives any of his children" — LOGOS. The MT has *mi-zar'o* (from his seed/offspring), a term covering the entire range of one's progeny. EMET's "seed" is literal but unusual in English when referring to actual children being sacrificed; LOGOS's "any of his children" captures the legal scope without agricultural awkwardness. PNEUMA's plain "his children" omits the *mi-* partitive ("from his," i.e., some of them) which LOGOS preserves with "any of."

**V. 2 — "Molech" transliteration.** All three agents preserve "Molech" as a proper name. Consensus: "Molech" — the standard English transliteration, retaining the MT's *la-Molekh* as a deity name, not a title. No agent glossed or explained the name in the translation text itself; explanatory material belongs in notes.

**V. 2 — "Stone him with stones" vs. "stone him."** EMET: "shall stone him with stones [ragom yirgemu oto ba-aven]." LOGOS: "shall stone him with stones." PNEUMA: "shall stone him." Consensus: "shall stone him with stones" — the *ba-aven* ("with stones") is present in the MT and should be preserved. PNEUMA drops "with stones," making the sentence smoother but losing the specificity of the execution method.

**V. 3 — "I myself will set my face."** EMET: "And I — I will set my face [ani eten et-panai]." LOGOS: "I myself will set my face against." PNEUMA: "I myself will set my face against." Consensus: "I myself will set my face against" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The emphatic *ani* pronoun ("I" standing separately) is captured by "I myself"; EMET's "And I — I will" through the dash-construction is accurate but less idiomatic for a consensus rendering. "I myself" is the standard formal solution to the emphatic Hebrew *ani* opening.

**V. 3 — "From his seed" vs. "one of his children."** EMET: "for from his seed he gave to the Molech." LOGOS: "because he has given one of his children to Molech." PNEUMA: "because he gave his children to Molech." Consensus: "because he has given one of his children to Molech" — LOGOS. The MT repeats the *mi-zar'o* (from his seed) construction from v. 2, confirming the sin being referenced. LOGOS's "one of his children" is the most natural English equivalent of the partitive "from his seed." PNEUMA again drops the partitive. EMET's "from his seed" is precise but unusual.

**V. 3 — "Defiling" vs. "to defile."** EMET: "so as to defile my sanctuary." LOGOS: "defiling my sanctuary." PNEUMA: "defiling my sanctuary." Consensus: "defiling my sanctuary" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT has a *le-* infinitive construct (*le-tame et-miqdashi*) expressing purpose or result; both "defiling" and "so as to defile" are defensible renderings. "Defiling" is more idiomatic in English and reads more naturally in the sentence without losing the causal force.

**V. 4 — "Close their eyes" vs. "hide their eyes."** EMET: "hide their eyes [ha'lem ya'limu]." LOGOS: "close their eyes." PNEUMA: "close their eyes" / "look the other way." Consensus: "close their eyes" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's "hide their eyes" is a more literal rendering of the *ha'lem* (hiphil of *alam*, "to conceal, hide") construction, but "close their eyes" is the natural English idiom for deliberate blindness and accurately conveys the meaning. The infinitive absolute *ha'lem ya'limu* ("hiding they shall hide") is captured by "at all close their eyes" in LOGOS, which is close to EMET but more idiomatic.

**V. 4 — "At all close" vs. plain "close."** EMET preserves the infinitive absolute force through "do at all hide." LOGOS: "at all close their eyes." PNEUMA: simply "close their eyes" / "look the other way." Consensus: "close their eyes" without "at all" — the emphatic infinitive absolute is important in formal-literal analysis but in a consensus text the plain form is cleaner. The note records the emphasis.

**V. 5 — "Family" vs. "clan."** EMET: "his family [mishpachto]." LOGOS: "his clan." PNEUMA: "his family." Consensus: "his family" — EMET/PNEUMA. *Mishpachah* denotes the extended family or kin-group and is consistently rendered "family" throughout the project. LOGOS's "clan" is defensible and is used in some modern translations (ESV), but "family" is the established project term for *mishpachah* and is more natural in English for the immediate household context here.

**V. 5 — "Those who go whoring after him" — cut-off clause structure.** EMET: "I will cut him off and all who go whoring after him [ha-zonim acharav], whoring after the Molech, from among their people." LOGOS: "will cut him off from among his people, him and all who follow him in whoring after Molech." PNEUMA: "I will cut him off, and all who prostitute themselves after him by chasing after Molech, I will cut them off from their people." Consensus: "I will cut him off from among his people — him and all who go whoring after him, whoring after Molech." The key decision: (a) "whoring" vs. "prostitute themselves" (PNEUMA) vs. "follow him in whoring" (LOGOS); (b) the repetition of "whoring after Molech." Ruling: "go whoring after" — the verb *zanah* in this idiomatic construction is the project's established rendering (cf. Hos; Ezek); "prostitute themselves" (PNEUMA) is more graphic in modern English but "whoring" is both accurate and project-standard. The double "whoring" in the MT — "whoring after him, whoring after Molech" — is preserved in the consensus to reflect the MT's rhetorical emphasis; LOGOS collapses the repetition.

**V. 6 — "Person" vs. "soul."** EMET: "the person [ha-nefesh]." LOGOS: "a person." PNEUMA: "anyone." Consensus: "a person" — LOGOS. *Ha-nefesh* is the standard "person" in legal formulas; EMET's definite "the person" is technically correct but reads oddly in English without an antecedent; "anyone" (PNEUMA) is colloquial and loses the legal subject register of *ha-nefesh*.

**V. 6 — "Mediums" and "spirits of the dead" vs. "necromancers" / "spiritists."** EMET: "the mediums [ha-ovot] and the spirits of the dead [ha-yid'onim]." LOGOS: "mediums and necromancers." PNEUMA: "mediums or spiritists." Consensus: "the mediums and the spirits of the dead" — EMET. *Ha-ovot* = "mediums/ghosts" and *ha-yid'onim* = "knowers/familiar spirits" (often translated "necromancers" or "spiritists"). The project established "mediums" for *ovot* at Lev 19:31. For *yid'onim*, LOGOS uses "necromancers" and PNEUMA uses "spiritists"; both are interpretive. "Spirits of the dead" (EMET) is more literal for *yid'onim* and is consistent with the note in v. 27 where EMET uses the same pairing. However, in v. 27 LOGOS uses "necromancer" and PNEUMA uses "spiritist." Ruling for the consensus across both v. 6 and v. 27: "mediums" for *ovot* and "spirits of the dead" for *yid'onim* — this preserves the distinction in the Hebrew between the medium-practitioner and the spirit-entity being consulted, and is the most literal rendering available. (See also v. 27 ruling.)

**V. 7 — "Sanctify yourselves" vs. "Consecrate yourselves."** EMET: "you shall sanctify yourselves [ve-hitqadishtem]." LOGOS: "Consecrate yourselves." PNEUMA: "Consecrate yourselves." Consensus: "you shall sanctify yourselves" — EMET. The Hithpael *hitqadishtem* (reflexive of *qadash*, "to make holy") is the same root as "holy" (*qedoshim*) in the same verse and "sanctifies you" (*meqadishkhem*) in v. 8. The triple occurrence of the *q-d-sh* root in vv. 7–8 — "sanctify yourselves... be holy... sanctifies you" — forms a deliberate semantic chain. LOGOS and PNEUMA use "Consecrate/consecrate," breaking the verbal link. "Sanctify yourselves" and "be holy" and "sanctifies you" preserve the root unity. (LOGOS and PNEUMA do use "sanctifies you" in v. 8.)

**V. 7 — "And be holy" vs. "be holy."** EMET: "and you shall be holy." LOGOS: "be holy." PNEUMA: "be holy." Consensus: "and be holy" — following the MT's *vi-heyitem qedoshim* which has the *vav*-conjunction. The coordinating conjunction is present in the Hebrew and should be represented.

**V. 8 — "Statutes" and "do them."** EMET: "you shall keep my statutes and you shall do them." LOGOS: "Keep my statutes and do them." PNEUMA: "Keep my statutes and carry them out." Consensus: "And you shall keep my statutes and do them" — the *vav* conjunction opening the verse (*ve-shemartem*) is present in MT. "Do them" is preferred over PNEUMA's "carry them out" — the Hebrew *va-asitem otam* is the plain *asah* ("to do/make"), not a compound. "Keep" for *shamar* is universal across all three agents.

**V. 9 — "Anyone who curses" vs. "any man who curses."** EMET: "any man who curses [ish ish asher yeqallel]." LOGOS: "anyone who curses." PNEUMA: "anyone who curses." Consensus: "anyone who curses" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT has *ish ish* (the doubled "man man" = "any man" distributive), which EMET renders as "any man." The ruling is that the distributive *ish ish* has generic force covering any person, and "anyone" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) captures this scope without the slight gender restriction implied by "any man" in modern English. This is a consistent project ruling.

**V. 9 — "His blood is upon him" vs. "his blood is on his own head."** EMET: "his blood is upon himself [damav bo]." LOGOS: "his blood is upon him." PNEUMA: "his blood is on his own head." Consensus: "his blood is upon him" — LOGOS. The MT formula *damav bo* is literally "his blood [is] upon him/in him." LOGOS's "his blood is upon him" is the standard formal rendering (cf. ESV, NASB, KJV). PNEUMA's "on his own head" is an interpretive paraphrase; "head" has no basis in the Hebrew *bo* ("upon him"). EMET's "upon himself" is accurate but slightly redundant since "his blood" already implies the reflexive. "Upon him" is cleaner. This formula recurs in vv. 11, 12, 13, 16, 27 and the consensus applies "their blood is upon them" consistently throughout.

**V. 10 — "With the wife of his neighbor" — first clause structure.** EMET: "a man who commits adultery with the wife of another man — who commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor." LOGOS: "If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor." PNEUMA: "If a man commits adultery with another man's wife — with the wife of his neighbor." Consensus: "If a man commits adultery with the wife of his neighbor" — LOGOS. The MT appears to have a slightly repetitive clause structure (*ish asher yina'ef et-eshet ish asher yina'ef et-eshet re'ehu*), which EMET represents with a dash-construction and PNEUMA partially preserves. LOGOS streamlines this by including "his neighbor" from the second clause and omitting the initial repetition — this is the standard approach in formal translation (ESV, NASB) and reads clearly without redundancy.

**V. 10 — "Both the adulterer and the adulteress."** All three agents agree: EMET "the adulterer and the adulteress"; LOGOS "both the adulterer and the adulteress"; PNEUMA "both the adulterer and the adulteress." Consensus: "both the adulterer and the adulteress" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. The MT names both (*ha-na'ef ve-ha-na'afet*); "both" makes the dual-execution explicit.

**V. 11 — "Lies with his father's wife."** EMET: "a man who lies with the wife of his father [eshet aviv]." LOGOS: "If a man lies with his father's wife." PNEUMA: "If a man sleeps with his father's wife." Consensus: "If a man lies with his father's wife" — LOGOS. "Lies with" is the standard formal equivalent for the Hebrew *shakav* in sexual contexts (throughout this chapter, the *shakav* formula is the consistent technical legal verb). PNEUMA's "sleeps with" is more colloquial and loses the legal register; "lies with" is universally used in the penal catalogue and should be consistent.

**V. 11 — "He has uncovered his father's nakedness."** EMET: "the nakedness of his father he has uncovered." LOGOS: "he has uncovered his father's nakedness." PNEUMA omits the nakedness formula here entirely ("he has violated his own father's honor"). Consensus: "he has uncovered his father's nakedness" — LOGOS. The project ruling is that *gilui ervah* formulas ("uncovering the nakedness of") are to be preserved throughout the penal code (terminology ruling). PNEUMA's "violated his own father's honor" is an interpretive paraphrase that eliminates the technical legal language; this is inadmissible for the consensus.

**V. 12 — "Perversion" for tevel.** EMET: "a confusion [tevel]." LOGOS: "a perversion." PNEUMA: "a perversion." Consensus: "a perversion" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. EMET's "confusion" is the etymological gloss on *tevel* (possibly from *balal*, "to mix/confuse"), but "perversion" better captures the English semantic field for a violation of created categorical order. "Perversion" is the established formal translation (ESV, NASB) and is retained.

**V. 13 — "Lies with a male as with a woman."** EMET: "a man who lies with a male [zachar] as one lies with a woman [mishkevei ishah]." LOGOS: "If a man lies with a male as with a woman." PNEUMA: "If a man lies with a man as with a woman." Consensus: "If a man lies with a male as with a woman" — LOGOS/EMET. The MT uses *zachar* (the sex-term "male," as in Gen 1:27) rather than *ish* (a man). LOGOS preserves "male" and EMET preserves "male." PNEUMA substitutes "man," losing the legal precision of the *zachar* term, which in this construction ("lying with a male") is the technical legal vocabulary. "Male" is the correct consensus rendering.

**V. 13 — "Both of them have committed an abomination."** EMET: "an abomination [to'evah] they have done, both of them." LOGOS: "both of them have committed an abomination." PNEUMA: "both of them have done something abominable." Consensus: "both of them have committed an abomination" — LOGOS. *To'evah* is the noun "abomination" and should be rendered as such; PNEUMA's "something abominable" weakens the noun to an adjective, losing the categorical force of the term. Word order follows LOGOS for natural English reading.

**V. 14 — "Takes a woman and her mother."** EMET: "a man who takes a woman and her mother [imah]." LOGOS: "If a man takes a woman and her mother also." PNEUMA: "If a man takes both a woman and her mother." Consensus: "If a man takes a woman and her mother also" — LOGOS. LOGOS's "also" reflects the MT's conjunctive structure; PNEUMA's "both" is an addition beyond the Hebrew.

**V. 14 — "He and they shall be burned" vs. "all three."** EMET: "in fire they shall burn him and them." LOGOS: "he and they shall be burned with fire." PNEUMA: "All three must be burned with fire." Consensus: "he and they shall be burned with fire" — LOGOS. The MT reads *oto ve-ethen yisrefu va-esh* ("him and them they shall burn in fire"), specifying "him and them" — which totals three people (the man, the woman, the mother). PNEUMA's "all three" is interpretively accurate but introduces a specification not in the Hebrew. LOGOS's "he and they" follows the MT exactly and is appropriately ambiguous about the number while conveying the fact that all parties are burned.

**V. 14 — Passive vs. active construction.** EMET: "in fire they shall burn him and them." LOGOS: "shall be burned with fire." PNEUMA: "must be burned with fire." Consensus: "shall be burned with fire" — LOGOS. The MT has an active construction (*yisrefu*, "they shall burn"), but the passive is conventional in English legal idiom and follows the pattern of "shall be put to death" throughout. LOGOS and PNEUMA both use passive.

**V. 15 — "Lies with an animal" vs. "has sexual relations."** EMET: "a man who gives his lying [shekhovto] to an animal." LOGOS: "If a man lies with an animal." PNEUMA: "If a man has sexual relations with an animal." Consensus: "If a man lies with an animal" — LOGOS. EMET's "gives his lying" (*shekhovto*, "his lying/intercourse") is literal for the Hebrew idiom but awkward in English. LOGOS's "lies with" is the established sexual sense of *shakav* and matches the pattern of the surrounding laws. PNEUMA's "has sexual relations with" is accurate but more circumlocutory than the Hebrew requires.

**V. 16 — "Approaches any animal so that it lies with her."** EMET: "a woman who approaches [tiqrav] any animal so that it lies [le-riv'ah] with her." LOGOS: "If a woman approaches any animal and lies with it." PNEUMA: "If a woman approaches any animal to mate with it." Consensus: "If a woman approaches any animal so that it lies with her" — EMET/adjusted. The MT has *le-riv'ah ota* ("for it to mount her / for lying with her") — the construction specifies the animal as the grammatical agent of the sexual act. LOGOS's "and lies with it" makes the woman the grammatical agent of *shakav*, reversing the Hebrew. PNEUMA's "to mate with it" is gender-neutral and loses the distinction. EMET's "so that it lies with her" preserves the MT's agency correctly. The consensus adopts EMET's construction here.

**V. 17 — "Takes his sister."** EMET: "a man who takes his sister [achoto]." LOGOS: "If a man takes his sister." PNEUMA: "If a man takes his sister." Consensus: standard — "If a man takes his sister" — all agree.

**V. 17 — "Sees her nakedness / she sees his nakedness."** All three agents preserve this double formula. EMET: "and sees her nakedness and she sees his nakedness." LOGOS: "and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness." PNEUMA omits the reciprocal clause and substitutes "they have sexual relations." Consensus: "and sees her nakedness, and she sees his nakedness" — EMET/LOGOS. The *gilui ervah* formula — here in the form of *seeing* nakedness rather than *uncovering* it — is a legal technical term and must be preserved. PNEUMA's omission of "nakedness" and substitution of "have sexual relations" eliminates the technical terminology.

**V. 17 — "It is a disgrace" for chesed.** EMET: "it is a disgrace [chesed hu]." LOGOS: "it is a shameful thing." PNEUMA: "that is a shameful thing." Consensus: "it is a disgrace" — EMET. This is the most contested lexical decision in the chapter. *Chesed* (normally "covenant-faithfulness / lovingkindness") appears here in a unique negative sense — "disgrace, shame, reproach." EMET: "disgrace." LOGOS: "shameful thing." PNEUMA: "shameful thing." The ruling is "disgrace" over "shameful thing" for two reasons: (1) "disgrace" as a noun more directly mirrors the Hebrew *chesed hu* (noun + pronoun predicate) than the adjectival "shameful thing"; (2) "disgrace" is the more established English rendering for this rare usage (cf. KJV "wickedness"; RSV/NRSV/ESV "disgrace"). The LXX's *atapon estin* ("it is improper/out of place") confirms the pejorative reading. The complete anomaly of *chesed* in this sense is recorded in the disagreement log.

**V. 17 — "Cut off in the sight of the children of their people."** EMET: "they shall be cut off [ve-nikhreitu] in the eyes of the sons of their people." LOGOS: "they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people." PNEUMA: "they must be publicly cut off from their people." Consensus: "they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people" — LOGOS. This is the unusual *karet* formula with *le-einei* ("before the eyes of / in the sight of"), a distinctive variant from the standard *nikhrat me-ammo* ("cut off from his people"). LOGOS preserves this verbatim. PNEUMA's "publicly cut off from their people" is an interpretive paraphrase; "publicly" is added beyond the Hebrew, and "their people" replaces "the children of their people" (*bney ammam*). The distinctive formula must be preserved as-is.

**V. 17 — "He has uncovered the nakedness of his sister."** EMET: "the nakedness of his sister he has uncovered — his iniquity he shall bear [avono yissa]." LOGOS: "He has uncovered his sister's nakedness; he shall bear his iniquity." PNEUMA: "He has violated his own sister — he will bear the guilt of it." Consensus: "He has uncovered the nakedness of his sister — he shall bear his iniquity" — EMET/LOGOS adjusted. The *gilui ervah* formula is preserved ("uncovered the nakedness of"). "Iniquity" (*avon*) is preferred over PNEUMA's "guilt" — *avon* is specifically iniquity/moral debt, a weightier term than "guilt." LOGOS uses "iniquity" correctly.

**V. 18 — "Menstrual impurity" vs. "sickness of her impurity."** EMET: "the sickness of her impurity [dovah]." LOGOS: "menstrual impurity." PNEUMA: "her monthly period." Consensus: "menstrual impurity" — LOGOS. The MT has *be-dovata* ("in her sickness/infirmity"), the root *dav* denoting illness or languishing, a term specifically associated with the menstrual state. "Menstrual impurity" (LOGOS) accurately renders the condition in idiomatic English. EMET's "sickness of her impurity" is more literal but awkward. PNEUMA's "monthly period" is too casual for the legal register and drops "impurity" (*tum'ah*).

**V. 18 — "He has laid bare her flow, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood."** All three agents preserve this double clause, though with minor variations. EMET: "her flow he has laid bare, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood." LOGOS: "he has laid bare her flow, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood." PNEUMA: "he has exposed the source of her flow and she has uncovered it." Consensus: "he has laid bare her flow, and she has uncovered the flow of her blood" — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "source of her flow" and "uncovered it" lose the parallel structure and the second mention of "flow of her blood" (*meqor dameha*) which LOGOS preserves. "Laid bare" (*hir'ah*) is correct for the first verb; "uncovered" (*galah*) for the second.

**V. 19 — "Your mother's sister or of your father's sister."** All three agents agree on the kinship terms. EMET: "the nakedness of your mother's sister and of your father's sister." LOGOS: "the nakedness of your mother's sister or of your father's sister." PNEUMA: "your mother's sister or your father's sister." Consensus: "the nakedness of your mother's sister or of your father's sister" — LOGOS. The *gilui ervah* formula is preserved. LOGOS's "or" (*o*) is correct — MT has *o* (or) between the two relations. EMET's "and" (*ve-*) may be reading the MT differently; the *o* (or) is more standard. PNEUMA omits "nakedness" from the prohibition formula.

**V. 19 — "That is to lay bare one's own flesh."** EMET: "for his own flesh [she'ero] he has laid bare." LOGOS: "for that is to lay bare one's own flesh." PNEUMA: "for that is to violate a close blood relative." Consensus: "for that is to lay bare one's own flesh" — LOGOS. The MT has *ki she'era gilla* ("for her/his flesh he has uncovered") — the word *she'er* means "flesh, near of kin." "Lay bare one's own flesh" (LOGOS) captures both the literal bodily sense and the kin-closeness. PNEUMA's "violate a close blood relative" is an interpretive paraphrase; *gilla* is specifically "uncover/lay bare," and *she'er* is literally "flesh."

**V. 20 — "Uncle's wife" / "his uncle's nakedness."** All three agents agree on the kinship terms: "uncle's wife" and "his uncle's nakedness." LOGOS: "he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness; they shall bear their sin and shall die childless." PNEUMA: "he has dishonored his uncle. They will bear the guilt — they will die childless." Consensus: "he has uncovered his uncle's nakedness; they shall bear their sin and shall die childless" — LOGOS. The *gilui ervah* formula is preserved. "Bear their sin" (*chet'am yis'u*) is preferred over PNEUMA's "bear the guilt" — *chet'* is specifically "sin," a more precise term than generic "guilt."

**V. 20 — "Shall die childless" for aririn yamut.** EMET: "childless [aririn] they shall die." LOGOS: "shall die childless." PNEUMA: "they will die childless." Consensus: "shall die childless" — LOGOS. *Ariri* (singular in MT) = one who dies without heirs; LOGOS's "shall die childless" is the standard formal rendering (cf. ESV). All three agents agree on "childless" for *ariri*; the word order "die childless" is more natural in English than EMET's "childless they shall die."

**V. 21 — "Takes his brother's wife" / "it is impurity."** EMET: "a man who takes the wife of his brother [eshet achiv] — it is impurity [niddah]." LOGOS: "If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity." PNEUMA: "If a man marries his brother's wife, it is an act of impurity." Consensus: "If a man takes his brother's wife, it is impurity" — LOGOS. PNEUMA's "marries" for *laqach* ("takes") is an interpretation that imports a formal marriage context not specified in the Hebrew — Leviticus uses *laqach* as the standard verb for taking a woman (sexually or in marriage), and here the context is prohibition, not specification of whether it is marriage. EMET's "the wife of his brother" vs. LOGOS's "his brother's wife" — LOGOS's genitive construction is more natural English.

**V. 21 — "He has uncovered his brother's nakedness — they shall be childless."** EMET: "the nakedness of his brother he has uncovered — childless they shall be." LOGOS: "he has uncovered his brother's nakedness — they shall be childless." PNEUMA: "He has violated his brother — they will be childless." Consensus: "he has uncovered his brother's nakedness — they shall be childless" — LOGOS. The *gilui ervah* formula preserved. PNEUMA again substitutes "violated" for "uncovered the nakedness of." *Arurim yihyu* (v. 21) is rendered "they shall be childless" — distinguished from v. 20's *ariri yamut* ("they shall die childless") by the absence of "die" here, following the MT distinction.

**V. 22 — "Statutes and rules" vs. "statutes and judgments."** EMET: "all my statutes and all my judgments [mishpatai]." LOGOS: "all my statutes and all my rules." PNEUMA: "all my statutes and all my ordinances." Consensus: "all my statutes and all my rules" — LOGOS. The Hebrew has *chuqqotai* (statutes, the term for laws whose rationale is not stated) and *mishpatai* (judgments/rulings, case-based laws). "Rules" (LOGOS/ESV) is the most common modern formal rendering of *mishpatim* in this context; "judgments" (EMET/KJV) is more archaic; "ordinances" (PNEUMA) is also standard. The project has not fixed a single term for *mishpatim*, but "rules" here follows the established ESV convention for Leviticus.

**V. 23 — "I detested them" vs. "I was loathed by them."** EMET: "I was loathed by them [va-aquts bam]." LOGOS: "I detested them." PNEUMA: "that is why I despised them." Consensus: "I detested them" — LOGOS. The MT has *va-aquts bam* — the verb *quts be-* means "to feel disgust/loathing toward" someone. The subject is YHWH, the object is the nations. EMET's "I was loathed by them" reads as if the nations loathed God (passive reading), but the Hebrew is active: God is the subject loathing/detesting the nations. LOGOS's "I detested them" is correct. PNEUMA's "I despised them" is also acceptable but "detested" is closer to the semantic field of *quts*, which implies physical revulsion.

**V. 24 — "Inherit" vs. "possess."** EMET: "You — you shall possess their ground [tirshу et-admatam]." LOGOS: "You shall inherit their land." PNEUMA: "you will possess their land." Consensus: "You shall inherit their land" — LOGOS. The MT verb is *yarash* (to possess, inherit, take possession of). LOGOS uses "inherit," EMET and PNEUMA use "possess" (or "possess their ground"). "Inherit" (LOGOS) captures the *yarash* connotation of taking as one's rightful inheritance; "possess" is more neutral. The project convention has used "possess/inheritance" in the Patriarchal land promises; for the verbal form here LOGOS's "inherit" is preferred as it captures the gift-character of the land.

**V. 24 — "Admatam" (ground) vs. "artzam" (land).** EMET renders *admatam* as "their ground" (the specific word *adamah* = ground/soil). LOGOS and PNEUMA render it as "their land." Consensus: "their land" — LOGOS/PNEUMA. While EMET's "ground" is technically correct for *adamah* (cf. Gen 2–3), in this land-inheritance context "their land" is more natural and is what the text means by *admatam*. The distinction between *adamah* and *eretz* is noted but does not require separate renderings at every occurrence.

**V. 24 — "Who has separated you" vs. "who distinguished you."** EMET: "who distinguished you [hivdalti etkhem] from the peoples." LOGOS: "who has separated you from the peoples." PNEUMA: "who has set you apart from all the peoples." Consensus: "who has separated you from the peoples" — LOGOS. The root *badal* (Hiphil) = "to separate, divide, distinguish." LOGOS uses "separated" consistently for *badal* throughout vv. 24–26, creating terminological unity with v. 26. EMET's "distinguished" is also accurate (and used in v. 25), but "separated" as the primary rendering for *hivdil* in the election formula is the more established rendering (ESV, NASB). PNEUMA adds "all" before "the peoples" — this addition is present in some LXX manuscripts (Vaticanus adds *panton ton ethnon*) but is not in the MT. The consensus follows MT without "all."

**V. 25 — "Distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean."** EMET: "you shall distinguish [ve-hivdaltem] between the clean animal and the unclean [ha-behemah ha-tehorah la-teme'ah] and between the unclean bird and the clean [ha-'of ha-tame le-tahor]." LOGOS: "you shall therefore distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean, and between the unclean bird and the clean." PNEUMA: "You must therefore distinguish between clean and unclean animals, and between clean and unclean birds." Consensus: "You shall therefore distinguish between the clean animal and the unclean, and between the unclean bird and the clean" — LOGOS. The MT's chiastic structure — clean animal/unclean, unclean bird/clean — is preserved by EMET and LOGOS but partially collapsed by PNEUMA. LOGOS's order is correct and natural.

**V. 25 — "Make yourselves detestable" vs. "make your souls detestable."** EMET: "you shall not make your souls detestable [lo teshaqtsu et-nafshoteikhem]." LOGOS: "You shall not make yourselves detestable." PNEUMA: "Do not defile yourselves." Consensus: "You shall not make yourselves detestable" — LOGOS. *Lo teshaqtsu et-nafshoteikhem* — the verb *shaqats* (Pi'el, "to make detestable/abominable") with *nafshoteikhem* ("your souls/selves") as the object. LOGOS's reflexive "make yourselves detestable" captures both the causative Pi'el and the reflexive soul-object. EMET's "your souls" is literal but "yourselves" is equivalent and more idiomatic. PNEUMA's "defile yourselves" is too weak — *shaqats* is stronger than *tame*; "detestable" preserves the *sheqets* connection.

**V. 25 — "Anything that creeps on the ground."** EMET: "anything that creeps on the ground [ha-romes al ha-adamah]." LOGOS: "anything with which the ground teems." PNEUMA: "anything that moves along the ground." Consensus: "anything that creeps on the ground" — EMET/PNEUMA. *Ha-romes* is the Qal participle of *ramas* ("to creep, move along the ground"), the word used for creeping/swarming creatures throughout Leviticus 11. "Creeps on the ground" is the established rendering for *romes* in this legal context. LOGOS's "with which the ground teems" introduces *shaqats* vocabulary ("swarming things") but the actual word here is *romes*; the paraphrase is too expansive.

**V. 26 — "Holy to me."** EMET: "you shall be holy to me [qedoshim tihyu li]." LOGOS: "You shall be holy to me." PNEUMA: "Be holy to me." Consensus: "And you shall be holy to me" — EMET/LOGOS with *vav*. The *vav* conjunction opening the verse is preserved.

**V. 27 — "In whom there is a medium or a spirit of the dead."** EMET: "a man or a woman in whom there is a medium [ov] or a spirit of the dead [yid'oni]." LOGOS: "A man or a woman who is a medium or a necromancer." PNEUMA: "Any man or woman who is a medium or a spiritist." Consensus: "A man or a woman in whom there is a medium or a spirit of the dead" — EMET. The MT has *asher yihyeh vahem ov o yid'oni* — "in whom there is an *ov* or a *yid'oni*." The phrase "in whom there is" is significant: it may imply possession (the spirit inhabits the practitioner) or professional identity. LOGOS and PNEUMA collapse this to "who is a medium / necromancer" — converting the possession/habitation idiom to a simple identity statement. EMET preserves the MT's phrasing. For the paired terms, the ruling from v. 6 applies: "medium" for *ov*, "spirit of the dead" for *yid'oni*.

**V. 27 — "Stoned with stones" vs. "they shall stone them."** EMET: "with stones they shall stone them [ba-aven yirgemu otam]." LOGOS: "They shall be stoned with stones." PNEUMA: "They are to be stoned." Consensus: "they shall be stoned with stones" — LOGOS. The MT has the active *yirgemu otam* ("they shall stone them") + *ba-aven* ("with stones"). LOGOS renders this as passive "shall be stoned with stones" (preserving "with stones" from *ba-aven*). PNEUMA drops "with stones." Following the parallel formula at v. 2 ("stone him with stones"), "with stones" is preserved in v. 27.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the threefold badal (vv. 24–26):** The root *badal* ("to separate/distinguish") appears three times in rapid succession: God has separated Israel from the nations (v. 24); Israel must distinguish between clean and unclean animals (v. 25); God has separated Israel to be his own (v. 26). The triple occurrence forms an explicit theological argument: divine election-separation (v. 24) → practical enacted distinction in daily life (v. 25) → the relational purpose of the separation (v. 26: *lihyot li*, "to be mine"). The consensus uses "separated" for the divine action (vv. 24, 26) and "distinguish" for the human practice (v. 25), following the root's range in English. The LXX uses three different Greek verbs for the same Hebrew root across vv. 24–26 (*diorizō*, *aphorizō*, *diastelō*), losing the terminological unity of the MT.

**On chesed (v. 17):** This is the single most linguistically anomalous term in the chapter. *Chesed* appears uniquely in a negative sense here — the only occurrence in the entire Hebrew Bible where the word clearly means "disgrace/shame" rather than "covenant-faithfulness/lovingkindness." The LXX (*atapon estin*, "it is improper/out of place"), the Peshitta, and rabbinic exegesis all confirm the pejorative reading. Some scholars propose a cognate Semitic root meaning "reproach"; others see irony (the act that should express *hesed* within the family instead violates it). The consensus preserves "disgrace" as the closest English equivalent and records the anomaly here.

**On karet (vv. 17, 18):** The *karet* ("cut off") penalty in this chapter occupies a middle tier between death (*mot yumat*) and childlessness. The MT formula varies: v. 17 uses *ve-nikhreitu le-einei bney ammam* ("they shall be cut off in the sight of the children of their people") — an unusual visible/public form of the formula; v. 18 uses the standard *ve-nikhreitu shenehem mi-qerev amam* ("both of them shall be cut off from among their people"). Both are rendered by their respective Hebrew formulas, with v. 17's distinctive *le-einei* construction preserved.

**On the land-vomiting image (v. 22):** The verb *qaya'* ("to vomit") applied to the land ejecting its inhabitants recurs from Lev 18:25, 28. The land is portrayed as a living moral subject that is nauseated by the accumulation of covenant violations. The LXX (Vaticanus) softens this to *prosochthizō* ("to be indignant/loathe"), replacing the visceral body-image with a more abstract emotional response. The consensus preserves "vomit you out" as the MT's deliberately arresting metaphor.

**On am ha-aretz (v. 2):** "The people of the land" designates the local community as the body obligated to carry out capital execution — not the priests or elders alone, but the entire community. The LXX (Vaticanus) replaces *am ha-aretz* with *synagoge Israēl* ("the congregation of Israel"), shifting from a geographic-communal designation to a formal cultic-institutional one. The consensus follows the MT: "the people of the land."

**On the medium/necromancer vocabulary (vv. 6, 27):** *Ovot* and *yid'onim* are the paired terms for illicit contact with the spirit world (cf. Lev 19:31; 1 Sam 28). *Ov* appears to denote the spirit itself, the medium who hosts it, or both; *yid'oni* ("knower") denotes a practitioner of knowledge derived from the dead. The LXX renders *ov* as *eggastrimythos* ("belly-speaker/ventriloquist") in v. 27, reflecting the ancient understanding of the medium's hollow, internalized voice (cf. Isa 29:4). The consensus uses "mediums" and "spirits of the dead" as the most literal available English pair, consistent with the project's practice of preserving legal-technical terminology.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Leviticus 20:1–27. All 27 verses rendered. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_020.md`.*
